clean_story: Learned traits passed down.  No DNA, just practice. "Skill inheritance" describes how abilities—like toolmaking or hunting techniques—spread across generations through teaching, imitation, or shared experience. It’s like copying a recipe by watching, not reading the book. No genes involved. Chimpanzees in Guinea crack nuts with stones—a skill youngsters learn by observing elders for years. Human examples?A blacksmith’s hammer techniques. A fisherman’s knot-tying. Even accents. Unlike instincts, inherited skills can vanish if not practiced. Japanese sword-forging nearly died when apprenticeships declined. Now, revivalists study old scrolls and scars on surviving blades to rebuild the lost steps. Three related subjects are cultural evolution, apprenticeship models and tool-making traditions.
